metadata.title:
NINE INCHES OF
rain IS ENOUGH


ON COLUMBIA

Washington Farmer Gets IS
Bushels Of Wheat to Acre


BY Dry Farming.

pages.raw_text:
| That wheat averaging IS bushels
an acre can be raised with nine
inches annual rainfall in the dry
country on the east side of the Co
lumbia river, in Douglas county,
Washington, is asserted by Frank
Thomas, a ranchman who belongs
to the Dry Farming congress, and
has written an account of the con-
ditions under which he is working.


"Our soil Is volcanic ASH"
writes Mr.. Thomas, with more or
less sand, inclined to drift with the
high winds. 1t ranges from two to
20 feet in depth. There is a clay
FtSsoil.


have been producing wheat five
yezrs under present conditions. We
raise as winter wheat, Jones Fife,
and as spring wheat, Bluestem.


II pillow seven inches deep, from
April Is to May 20. We get our
greatest rainfall in the winter, from
November Is to February is.


Last year l produced IS bushels
of wheat to the acre, with nine
inches rainfall.


Il am not rotating my crops, as
the only marketable crop at present
Is wheat. Our method of summer
tillage makes 1t possible only to
raise one crop in two years and
whether this Ia depicting the pro-
ductiveness of the soil have been
able, as yet, to form no opinion"


Mr.. Thomas is deeply interest in
the work the Dry Farming congress
is doing. He expects to attend the
Fifth Congress at Spokane and ex-
hibit some Of dry grown wheat at
the International Dry Farming ex-
position.
